solution: 
```cpp
class Solution {
public:
    int maxAbsValExpr(vector<int>& arr1, vector<int>& arr2) {
        int n = arr1.size();
        int maxVal = 0;
        int max1 = INT_MIN, min1 = INT_MAX;
        int max2 = INT_MIN, min2 = INT_MAX;
        int max3 = INT_MIN, min3 = INT_MAX;
        int max4 = INT_MIN, min4 = INT_MAX;

        for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
            int a = arr1[i] + arr2[i];
            int b = arr1[i] - arr2[i];
            int c = -arr1[i] + arr2[i];
            int d = -arr1[i] - arr2[i];

            max1 = max(max1, a + i);
            min1 = min(min1, a + i);
            max2 = max(max2, a - i);
            min2 = min(min2, a - i);
            max3 = max(max3, b + i);
            min3 = min(min3, b + i);
            max4 = max(max4, c + i);
            min4 = min(min4, c + i);
        }

        maxVal = max(maxVal, max1 - min1);
        maxVal = max(maxVal, max2 - min2);
        maxVal = max(maxVal, max3 - min3);
        maxVal = max(maxVal, max4 - min4);

        return maxVal;
    }
};
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(n)
space complexity: O(1)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution leverages the properties of absolute values and simplifies the expression into four different linear combinations. By tracking the maximum and minimum values of these combinations across the array, we can compute the maximum value of the original expression in linear time. This approach is efficient and avoids the need for a brute-force O(n^2) solution.
